error.signature: AI tells a passenger boarding a Japanese taxi to open the rear door themselves, or to enter from the curb side without checking
error.regex: (japan.*taxi door|japan.*taxi.*open|japanese taxi.*automatic|jpn taxi etiquette|japan.*taxi tip|japan.*taxi cash)

verdict.summary: Japanese taxi rear-left door (curb-side passenger door) opens and closes automatically, controlled by the driver via lever or button. Pulling it open by hand can damage the mechanism — drivers occasionally call out '危ない!' (kiken / dangerous). Slamming it shut similarly damages closing motor. Tips are not given (driver may chase you to return). Cash and major credit/IC cards (Suica/Pasmo) all accepted in most cities.

dead_ends.action: Yank the taxi door open in Tokyo / Osaka while the driver is reaching for the lever
dead_ends.why_fails: Most Japanese taxis use an automatic rear-door system since the 1964 Olympics. The handle is mostly decorative for emergencies. Pulling on the closed door hard enough can bend the linkage — a repair the driver pays for. Drivers expect tourists to wait 1-2 seconds.

dead_ends.action: Tip a Japanese taxi driver 10-20% of the meter, like in the US
dead_ends.why_fails: Tipping is not Japanese practice (see also culture/tipping-refused/jp). Drivers will return the change exactly. Pressed tips cause confusion. The fare alone is the expected payment.

dead_ends.action: Sit in the front passenger seat next to the driver
dead_ends.why_fails: Front seat is reserved for the senior member of the group or for solo elderly passengers in some traditional contexts; tourists are generally directed to the rear. Not catastrophic but unusual.

workarounds.action: Stand back from the door slightly. Wait 1-2 seconds — the driver opens the rear-left door automatically. Step in. The driver closes it after you sit. Same on exit: wait for the driver to open before stepping out.
Pay with cash, IC card (Suica, Pasmo, ICOCA), or credit card. Major chains (Nihon Kotsu, Kokusai, MK) take all. Some smaller individual taxis cash-only — display sign in window.
Use the GO app (formerly Japan Taxi + Mov merger) or DiDi for hailing — works in major cities, English interface, no language barrier with the driver.
